Version.Name: Carcinogenicity oral Slope Factor model (IRFMN)
Version.Summary: Quantitative model for the carcinogenicity (oral route) Slope Factor.

Guide.Description: The model is a Multi-layer perceptron – artificial neural networks (MLP-ANNs) that provides prediction for the oral slope factor (OSF) (Expert assessment based on presence of carcinogenic effects in rodents).
Guide.Dataset: The model is based on a dataset of including 315 compounds with OSF data from the Risk Assessment Information System (RAIS) Toxicity values database (https://rais.ornl.gov). Data cover different pollutants categories including organic and inorganic compounds such as dioxins, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs, pesticides and metals frequently found in contaminated sites.1110 values were retrieved for oral slope factor (OSF, mg/kg-day)-1). Only compounds with continuous data were used for modelling. OSF values were converted in logarithmic units for modelling purposes. Canonical SMILES were retrieved for each chemical from two sources (JChem for Office and ChemID plus) then chemicals showing incongruences between the various sources were rejected. Most of the QSAR models cannot handle inorganic compounds, metals and metal complexes, organic salts and data related to mixtures and these compounds have been also rejected. Ionized structures were neutralized and counterions eliminated. The datasets were further checked for the presence of duplicates. The entire data set was randomly split into a training set (TrS) and a test set (TS) containing the 80% and 20% of the modeling set respectively.
Guide.insilico.dilibayer.Descriptors:
The model is based on a Multi-layer perceptron – artificial neural network (MLP-ANN) implementation in KNIME.
The model uses the following descriptors:
[1]nCIC number of rings (cyclomatic number) [2]GATS2s Geary autocorrelation of lag 2 weighted by I-state [3]P_VSA_m_5 P_VSA-like on mass, bin 5 [4]SM08_EA(bo) spectral moment of order 8 from edge adjacency mat. weighted by bond order [5]nRNNOx number of N-nitroso groups (aliphatic) [6]nFuranes number of Furanes [7]N-067 Al2-NH [8]CATS2D_03_DA CATS2D Donor-Acceptor at lag 03 [9]CATS2D_04_AL CATS2D Acceptor-Lipophilic at lag 04 [10]B05[O-O] Presence/absence of O - O at topological distance 5 [11]B08[Cl-Cl] Presence/absence of Cl - Cl at topological distance 8 [12]F04[O-Cl] Frequency of O - Cl at topological distance 4
The descriptors have been generated with VEGA software using CDK libraries, while feature selection was performed with the “gaselect” R package that implements partial least square - genetic algorithm (PLS-GA) and repeated double cross-validation for statistical evaluation of subset of descriptors. The following settings were applied for the PLS-GA: initial population 2000; number of iterations 5000; minimum number of variable 5; maximum number of variable 12. Optimal subsets of descriptors returned by the final iteration of the run were used for model derivation, using R2 as fitness function.

AD.Name: Similar_Molecules
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: strongly similar compounds with known experimental value in the training set have been found
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: only moderately similar compounds with known experimental value in the training set have been found
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: no similar compounds with known experimental value in the training set have been found

AD.Name: Accuracy
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: accuracy of prediction for similar molecules found in the training set is good
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: accuracy of prediction for similar molecules found in the training set is not optimal
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: accuracy of prediction for similar molecules found in the training set is not adequate

AD.Name: Concordance
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: similar molecules found in the training set have experimental values that agree with the target compound predicted value
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: similar molecules found in the training set have experimental values that slightly disagree with the target compound predicted value
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: similar molecules found in the training set have experimental values that completely disagree with the target compound predicted value

AD.Name: Maximum_Error
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: the maximum error in prediction of similar molecules found in the training set has a low value, considering the experimental variability
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: the maximum error in prediction of similar molecules found in the training set has a moderate value, considering the experimental variability
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: the maximum error in prediction of similar molecules found in the training set has a high value, considering the experimental variability

AD.Name: Model_Descriptors_Range_Check
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: descriptors for this compound have values inside the descriptor range of the compounds of the training set
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: descriptors for this compound have values outside the descriptor range of the compounds of the training set

AD.Name: ACF_SimCheck
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: all atom centered fragment of the compound have been found in the compounds of the training set
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: some atom centered fragment of the compound have not been found in the compounds of the training set or are rare fragments
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: a prominent number of atom centered fragments of the compound have not been found in the compounds of the training set or are rare fragments

AD.Name: AD_Index
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: predicted substance is into the Applicability Domain of the model
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: predicted substance could be out of the Applicability Domain of the model
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: predicted substance is out of the the Applicability Domain of the model

xml.Reference:
The model has been developed by: [1] Alberto Manganaro Kode srl info@kode-solutions.net [2] Emilio Benfenati Istituto di Ricerche Farmacologiche Mario Negri IRCCS emilio.benfenati@marionegri.it
It has been described here: [1] Toma, C., Manganaro, A., Raitano, G., Marzo, M., Gadaleta, D., Baderna, D., ... and Benfenati, E. (2021). QSAR Models for Human Carcinogenicity: An Assessment Based on Oral and Inhalation Slope Factors. Molecules, 26(1), 127. [2] Benfenati E, Roncaglioni A, Lombardo A, Manganaro A. Integrating QSAR, Read-Across, and Screening Tools: The VEGAHUB Platform as an Example. Advances in Computational Toxicology: Springer; 2019. p. 365-81.
For a general description of the VEGA software see: Benfenati E, Manganaro A, Gini G VEGA-QSAR: AI inside a platform for predictive toxicology Proceedings of the workshop "Popularize Artificial Intelligence 2013", December 5th 2013, Turin, Italy Published on CEUR Workshop Proceedings Vol-1107
xml.Endpoint: The endpoint is the Oral Slope Factor (OSF) expressed in base 10 logaritmic units (logOSF, 1/(mg/kg-day)). Different species (rodents).
